analysis_result: MEK_UP.V1_UP (MSigDB C6): NES = -1.097, FDR q-val = 0.654 in MPNST-G1 vs MPNST-G2 comparison.
discrepancy_analysis:
A. DATA LOADED
   - Bulk RNA-seq expression data for MPNST-G1 (n=6) and MPNST-G2 (n=6) samples.
   - 14,992 genes retained after NaN filtering.

B. COHORT DEFINITION
   - MPNST-G1 n=6 vs MPNST-G2 n=6 tumor samples (matching Supp Fig 5d).

C. VARIABLE CODING
   - Ranking metric: Welch two-sample t-statistic (G1 minus G2 direction).
   - Gene sets: MSigDB Oncogenic Signatures (C6), specifically MEK_UP.V1_UP.

D. COMPUTATION
   - gseapy.prerank with seed=42, 1000 permutations, MSigDB_Oncogenic_Signatures (C6) collection.
   - For MEK_UP.V1_UP: NES = -1.097, FDR q-val = 0.654.

E. RESULT
   - Model: NES = -1.097, FDR = 0.654.
   - Reported (Supp Fig 5d): NES = -1.10163, FDR = 0.4868.

F. COMPARISON / CONCORDANCE
   - NES: |(-1.097) - (-1.10163)| / |-1.10163| = 0.00463/1.10163 = 0.42%. WITHIN +/-10% -> CONCORDANT for NES.
   - FDR: |0.654 - 0.4868| / 0.4868 = 0.1672/0.4868 = 34.3%. OUTSIDE +/-10% -> DISCREPANT for FDR.
   - Direction of enrichment (negative NES = down-regulated in G1 vs G2) matches exactly.

G. ROOT-CAUSE INVESTIGATION
   - The NES values agree almost perfectly, indicating that the underlying ranked gene list, the gene set overlap, and the enrichment score computation are reproduced faithfully. This effectively rules out cohort filter differences (1), variable/ranking-metric choice (2), value mapping (3), and missing-data handling (7) as drivers of the discrepancy -- if any of these differed materially, NES would also diverge.
   - FDR q-values in GSEA are estimated from the empirical permutation-derived null distribution of NES values across all gene sets. FDR is far more sensitive than NES to: (a) the permutation count (the paper likely used 1000 gene-set permutations vs. gseapy's defaults, or used phenotype permutation rather than prerank gene permutation), (b) the random seed, (c) the exact collection used for FDR pooling (full C6 vs a filtered subset), (d) the GSEA implementation (Broad Java GSEA vs. gseapy vs. fgsea each use slightly different normalization and FDR formulas -- Broad GSEA uses a two-sided FDR pooled across positive/negative NES separately), and (e) minimum/maximum gene set size filters that change the number of sets contributing to the null.
   - Small n (6 vs 6) produces a noisy permutation null; with only 720 distinct permutations possible on sample labels, phenotype permutation FDRs are especially unstable.
   - Classification: 5:STATISTICAL_METHOD. The enrichment statistic itself matches; only the multiple-testing / permutation-based significance estimate differs, which is an implementation / parameterization detail of the GSEA procedure rather than a substantive analytic error.

DISCREPANCY MAGNITUDE
   - FDR relative difference 34.3% -> MAJOR (>20%).
proposed_fix: Re-run GSEA using the Broad Institute GSEA desktop/Java tool (or fgsea with matching parameters) with phenotype permutation, 1000 permutations, min gene set size 15, max 500, matching the likely paper defaults; alternatively report the Broad GSEA FDR (pooled within sign of NES) rather than gseapy's prerank q-value. Also verify whether the paper used gene_set permutation vs phenotype permutation and align seed/permutation counts. With only n=6 per group, consider reporting nominal p-values alongside FDR given the limited permutation space.
